description:
You are a monitoring specialist who has just received two plain-text files
  under /home/user/data/ that were exported by an old script.  
  1. /home/user/data/hosts.txt – one hostname per line.  
  2. /home/user/data/metrics.txt – matching positional lines that look like  
     cpu=<number>␠mem=<number>

  Your goal is to do three things using classic shell utilities that perform
  column manipulation (think cut, paste, etc.).  No line-editing with an
  interactive editor is necessary.

  A) Build /home/user/data/host_cpu.csv containing exactly two columns:
     • column-1  = the hostname from hosts.txt  
     • column-2  = the numeric CPU percentage that appears after “cpu=” in
                   metrics.txt  
     • separator = a single comma “,” (no spaces before/after the comma)  
     • file must end with a single trailing newline

     Expected example of the *entire* resulting file (content must match
     exactly; automated tests will diff):

       web01,23
       web02,87
       db01,55
       cache01,92

  B) From that CSV, extract all lines whose CPU value is 80 or higher and
     write them – in the same order they appear – to
     /home/user/data/high_cpu_alerts.log.  Format for each alert line must be:

       ALERT: <hostname> CPU at <number>%

     Do not add extra spaces, punctuation, blank lines, or a header.  
     The file must also end with a single trailing newline.

     Expected final content:

       ALERT: web02 CPU at 87%
       ALERT: cache01 CPU at 92%

  C) Display to stdout the line count of host_cpu.csv so that the supervising
     engineer sees quick confirmation in the terminal.

  Restrictions & hints:
  • Accomplish everything with a *maximum* of four simple one-liners; no
    temporary interactive editing.  
  • All tooling must be from the POSIX coreutils family (cut, paste, grep,
    awk, etc.); nothing exotic is required.  
  • Neither root access nor sudo is available, so stay in /home/user.  
  • The automated test will check:  
      – exact paths of the two output files.  
      – byte-for-byte match of their contents including the final newlines.  
      – that the command you run prints a single integer (the line count) to
        stdout.

  Before you begin, verify that /home/user/data/hosts.txt and
  /home/user/data/metrics.txt already exist and contain four lines each as
  mentioned above.  Feel free to `cat` them first for orientation, then
  proceed with the necessary column extraction, joining, filtering, and final
  count display.
truth:
Existing seed files:

  1. /home/user/data/hosts.txt
     Contents exactly (with trailing newline):
       web01
       web02
       db01
       cache01

  2. /home/user/data/metrics.txt
     Contents exactly (with trailing newline):
       cpu=23 mem=40
       cpu=87 mem=66
       cpu=55 mem=72
       cpu=92 mem=31

  Files that must be created by the agent:

  A) /home/user/data/host_cpu.csv
     Expected final contents (with trailing newline):
       web01,23
       web02,87
       db01,55
       cache01,92

  B) /home/user/data/high_cpu_alerts.log
     Expected final contents (with trailing newline):
       ALERT: web02 CPU at 87%
       ALERT: cache01 CPU at 92%

  Test harness will also capture stdout from the agent’s session and assert
  that, after the creation of host_cpu.csv, a single line reading “4” (the
  CSV line count) appears.
